title: Pressure Injury Staging, Braden-Based Risk Assessment, a Prevention Bundle, and Stage-Appropriate Wound Care in an Immobile 78-Year-Old Recovering from Stroke (Sacral Stage 2 Injury + Heel Deep-Tissue Pressure Injury, Braden 12): Correctly Staging Injuries, Repositioning and Offloading, Moisture and Nutrition Optimization, Moist Wound Healing, and Preventing Progression
subtitle: A PRESSURE INJURY (formerly pressure ulcer/decubitus) is localized damage to the skin and/or underlying soft tissue, usually over a BONY PROMINENCE (sacrum, coccyx, ischium, greater trochanter, heels, occiput, elbows) or related to a MEDICAL DEVICE, caused by sustained PRESSURE (and often SHEAR), which occludes capillaries and produces ischemic tissue injury. RISK FACTORS: IMMOBILITY/inactivity, impaired SENSORY perception (can't feel/respond to discomfort — e.g., stroke, spinal injury, sedation, neuropathy), MOISTURE (incontinence, perspiration, wound drainage), poor NUTRITION/HYDRATION (low protein/albumin, dehydration), FRICTION and SHEAR (sliding/dragging in bed), impaired PERFUSION/oxygenation, advanced AGE (thin fragile skin), altered mental status, and devices (oxygen tubing, catheters, casts → device-related pressure injuries). RISK ASSESSMENT: use a validated tool such as the BRADEN SCALE — six subscales (SENSORY PERCEPTION, MOISTURE, ACTIVITY, MOBILITY, NUTRITION, FRICTION/SHEAR); LOWER total score = HIGHER risk (roughly: ≤18 at risk; 15-18 mild, 13-14 moderate, 10-12 high, ≤9 very high) — assess on admission, with any change, and at regular intervals, and target interventions to the deficient subscales. STAGING (NPIAP), staged by the DEEPEST visible tissue level: STAGE 1 — INTACT skin with localized NON-BLANCHABLE ERYTHEMA (in darkly pigmented skin, blanching may not be visible, so assess for color change vs surrounding skin, warmth/coolness, firmness/edema, or pain); STAGE 2 — PARTIAL-thickness skin loss with exposed DERMIS: a shallow, moist, pink/red wound bed OR an intact or ruptured SERUM-filled blister; NO slough/eschar, no visible fat (it is NOT used for skin tears, moisture-associated dermatitis, tape injuries, or burns); STAGE 3 — FULL-thickness skin loss with visible SUBCUTANEOUS FAT; slough and/or eschar may be present but do NOT obscure the depth; may have undermining and tunneling; NO exposed bone/tendon/muscle; STAGE 4 — FULL-thickness skin AND TISSUE loss with exposed or directly palpable BONE, TENDON, MUSCLE, fascia, ligament, or cartilage; slough/eschar may be present; often with undermining/tunneling (risk of osteomyelitis); UNSTAGEABLE — full-thickness loss in which the wound base is OBSCURED by SLOUGH and/or ESCHAR, so the true depth/stage cannot be determined until enough is removed (KEY EXCEPTION: STABLE — dry, adherent, intact, without erythema/fluctuance — ESCHAR on the HEELS or an ischemic limb serves as 'the body's natural biological cover' and should NOT be removed); DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (DTPI) — intact or non-intact skin with localized persistent NON-BLANCHABLE DEEP RED, MAROON, or PURPLE discoloration, or an epidermal separation revealing a dark wound bed or a BLOOD-filled blister — resulting from intense/prolonged pressure-and-shear at the bone-muscle interface; it may resolve or evolve RAPIDLY to reveal the true extent of injury. IMPORTANT: do NOT 'reverse/back-stage' (a healing Stage 4 is documented as a 'healing Stage 4,' not re-labeled Stage 2, because the lost muscle/fat/dermis is replaced by granulation/scar, not original tissue). PREVENTION BUNDLE (pressure injuries are LARGELY PREVENTABLE; hospital-acquired Stage 3/4 are considered serious reportable 'never' events): (1) comprehensive SKIN ASSESSMENT head-to-toe on admission and at least daily (and at every reposition), INCLUDING under and around medical devices; (2) Braden RISK ASSESSMENT and individualized care; (3) REPOSITION on a schedule — at least every 2 hours in bed and every 1 hour in a chair (individualize), using a turn schedule; (4) PRESSURE-REDISTRIBUTING SUPPORT SURFACES (specialty foam/alternating-pressure/low-air-loss mattress and chair cushions) — and DO NOT use ring/'donut' cushions (they cause ischemia); (5) FLOAT THE HEELS off the bed (place a pillow lengthwise under the calves so the heels are suspended); (6) keep the HEAD OF BED at or below 30 degrees when feasible to reduce SHEAR; (7) MOISTURE management — prompt incontinence care, gentle cleansing, MOISTURE-BARRIER products, absorbent pads that wick moisture (treat incontinence-associated dermatitis, which is NOT a pressure injury); (8) minimize FRICTION/SHEAR — LIFT (use draw/slide sheets and lifts) rather than DRAG, low-friction textiles, manage spasticity; (9) optimize NUTRITION and HYDRATION — adequate PROTEIN and calories, vitamins/minerals (and a dietitian referral; low albumin/poor intake impair healing); (10) early MOBILITY/activity as able; (11) avoid MASSAGING reddened areas/bony prominences (worsens tissue damage); (12) pad/rotate/reposition medical devices. STAGE-APPROPRIATE WOUND CARE: maintain a clean, MOIST wound bed (moist wound healing) while keeping the SURROUNDING (periwound) skin DRY/protected; cleanse with normal saline or a noncytotoxic wound cleanser (do NOT routinely use cytotoxic agents like full-strength hydrogen peroxide/Dakin's/povidone-iodine on healing tissue); select dressings by stage/exudate/depth (e.g., transparent film or hydrocolloid for Stage 1-2; hydrocolloid/foam for Stage 2-3; hydrogel for dry wounds, calcium ALGINATE/foam for heavy exudate, fillers for cavities/undermining); DEBRIDE nonviable tissue (slough/eschar) for Stage 3/4 and unstageable wounds (sharp, enzymatic, autolytic, or mechanical) — EXCEPT stable dry heel eschar; assess for and manage INFECTION (increasing erythema/warmth/odor/purulence/pain, systemic signs; osteomyelitis with exposed bone) and EXUDATE; OFFLOAD pressure from the wound (the wound will NOT heal under continued pressure); manage PAIN; involve a WOUND/OSTOMY (WOC) nurse and specialists; and DOCUMENT precisely (location, stage, dimensions length x width x DEPTH, undermining/tunneling, wound-bed tissue type %, exudate amount/type, periwound skin, odor, pain), measure, and photograph per policy, reassessing regularly. NURSING ROLE: assess skin and Braden risk, STAGE injuries correctly (and recognize DTPI/unstageable + the stable-heel-eschar exception + no back-staging), implement the comprehensive PREVENTION bundle (reposition, support surfaces, float heels, moisture + nutrition + friction/shear control), deliver stage-appropriate moist wound care with offloading, prevent and detect infection, optimize nutrition, and document/coordinate care to PREVENT new injuries and promote healing

patient.name: M. Sokolov
patient.code: Full code; recovering from a recent ischemic stroke with left hemiparesis; goal = heal the existing pressure injury, prevent progression/new injuries, and restore mobility/nutrition
patient.admitDate: Recovering on a rehabilitation/medical unit ~2 weeks after an ischemic stroke with left hemiparesis. She has LIMITED MOBILITY (bed- and chair-bound, requires assistance to reposition), impaired sensation/awareness on the affected side, urinary INCONTINENCE, and POOR ORAL INTAKE with recent weight loss. A skin assessment reveals a SACRAL pressure injury (shallow open area with a pink-red moist wound bed, no slough) and a dark maroon, non-blanchable area on the right HEEL. Her BRADEN score is 12 (high risk). The nurse must correctly STAGE the injuries, implement a comprehensive prevention bundle, optimize moisture/nutrition, and provide stage-appropriate wound care to prevent progression.
patient.room: Rehabilitation/medical unit; pressure-redistributing mattress + chair cushion; turn schedule posted; wound/ostomy (WOC) nurse + dietitian + PT/OT engaged

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; SACRAL injury = partial-thickness, shallow open ulcer with a pink-red MOIST wound bed, no slough/eschar, no visible fat → STAGE 2; right HEEL = intact skin with persistent NON-BLANCHABLE DEEP MAROON/PURPLE discoloration → DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (DTPI); periwound sacral skin macerated from incontinence (incontinence-associated dermatitis, distinct from the pressure injury)

37.0°C; BRADEN total 12 (HIGH risk) — low subscale scores for mobility, activity, sensory perception (stroke), moisture (incontinence), and nutrition (poor intake/weight loss); friction/shear a problem with transfers → individualized prevention targeted to each deficient area

37.0°C; comprehensive bundle: reposition q2h (turn schedule) + q1h in chair; pressure-redistributing mattress/cushion (NO donut/ring); HEELS FLOATED off the bed (pillow under calves); HOB ≤30 degrees to reduce shear; LIFT not drag (draw sheet/lift); prompt incontinence care + moisture-barrier cream; dietitian for protein/calorie optimization; early mobility with PT/OT; no massaging of reddened/bony areas; device sites checked

37.0°C; SACRAL Stage 2: cleanse with normal saline, maintain a MOIST wound bed with an appropriate dressing (e.g., hydrocolloid/foam) + protect periwound skin (barrier) + offload pressure; HEEL DTPI: OFFLOAD/float + protect + monitor closely (may evolve); avoid cytotoxic cleansers on healing tissue; WOC nurse co-managing; documented size/depth/tissue/exudate + measured + photographed per policy

36.9°C; sacral Stage 2 improving (clean granulating bed, decreasing size) with offloading + moist wound care + improved nutrition + continence management; heel DTPI stable, skin intact, no progression with consistent heel offloading; no new injuries; Braden interventions sustained; weight/intake improving with dietitian plan

—; teaching contrast — without consistent repositioning/offloading, moisture/nutrition management, and heel floating, the sacral Stage 2 could deepen to Stage 3/4 and the heel DTPI could rapidly evolve to an unstageable/full-thickness wound; hospital-acquired Stage 3/4 injuries are largely preventable serious reportable events

labs.v: 78 y/o woman M. Sokolov recovering on a rehab/medical unit ~2 weeks after an ISCHEMIC STROKE with left hemiparesis. She has multiple PRESSURE-INJURY RISK FACTORS that cluster after stroke: IMMOBILITY (bed- and chair-bound, needs assistance to reposition), impaired SENSORY perception/awareness on the affected side (can't sense/respond to discomfort), urinary INCONTINENCE (MOISTURE), POOR ORAL INTAKE with recent weight loss (impaired NUTRITION/low protein), FRICTION/SHEAR risk during transfers, and advanced AGE with thin, fragile skin. A skin assessment reveals (1) a SACRAL injury — a shallow, open, partial-thickness ulcer with a pink-red moist wound bed and no slough/eschar — and (2) a right HEEL area of intact skin with persistent NON-BLANCHABLE DEEP MAROON/PURPLE discoloration; the sacral periwound skin is macerated from incontinence. Her BRADEN score is 12 (HIGH risk). PMH: ischemic stroke (left hemiparesis), hypertension, mild cognitive changes. SOCIAL: widowed; family involved; previously lived independently; now requires significant assistance. The nursing priorities: STAGE the injuries correctly, perform a Braden-based risk assessment, implement a comprehensive PREVENTION bundle, optimize moisture/nutrition, and provide stage-appropriate WOUND CARE — recognizing that pressure injuries are largely PREVENTABLE and that prompt, correct care prevents progression and promotes healing. Pressure-injury care is a core Basic-Care-&-Comfort competency (skin integrity, hygiene, nutrition, mobility, comfort).
labs.r: Stroke clusters pressure-injury risk factors: immobility + impaired sensation + incontinence (moisture) + poor nutrition + friction/shear + age. She has a sacral injury, a heel deep-tissue injury, and a high Braden (12). RN role: stage correctly + Braden risk assessment + prevention bundle + nutrition/moisture management + stage-appropriate wound care.

labs.n: Staging (NPIAP) + deep tissue injury + unstageable + no back-staging
labs.v: STAGE a pressure injury by the DEEPEST visible viable tissue layer: STAGE 1 — INTACT skin with localized NON-BLANCHABLE ERYTHEMA (press the area: it does NOT blanch white; in darkly pigmented skin blanching may be hard to see, so compare color to surrounding skin and assess warmth/coolness, firmness/boggyness/edema, and pain); STAGE 2 — PARTIAL-thickness loss exposing the DERMIS: a shallow, moist, pink/red wound bed OR an intact or ruptured SERUM-FILLED blister; NO slough, NO eschar, NO visible subcutaneous fat (Stage 2 is NOT used to describe skin tears, tape/medical-adhesive injuries, incontinence-/moisture-associated dermatitis, or burns); STAGE 3 — FULL-thickness loss with visible SUBCUTANEOUS FAT; slough/eschar may be present without obscuring depth; possible undermining/tunneling; bone/tendon/muscle NOT exposed; STAGE 4 — FULL-thickness skin AND tissue loss with exposed/palpable BONE, TENDON, MUSCLE (or fascia/ligament/cartilage); slough/eschar may be present; frequent undermining/tunneling; osteomyelitis risk; UNSTAGEABLE — full-thickness loss where SLOUGH/ESCHAR OBSCURES the base, so the depth/stage is unknown until enough is removed (CRITICAL EXCEPTION: STABLE — dry, adherent, intact, no erythema/fluctuance — ESCHAR on the HEEL or an ischemic limb should NOT be debrided; it is the body's natural biological cover); DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (DTPI) — intact or non-intact skin with persistent NON-BLANCHABLE DEEP RED/MAROON/PURPLE discoloration or a blood-filled blister, from pressure/shear at the bone-muscle interface, which may resolve or rapidly DETERIORATE to reveal the true (often full-thickness) injury. DO NOT REVERSE/BACK-STAGE: as a deep injury heals it fills with granulation/scar tissue, NOT the original muscle/fat/dermis, so a healing Stage 4 is documented as a 'HEALING Stage 4' (or 'Stage 4 in granulation'), never re-labeled as a lower stage. Distinguish pressure injuries from look-alikes: MOISTURE-ASSOCIATED SKIN DAMAGE/incontinence-associated dermatitis (diffuse, in skin folds/perineum, from moisture — not over a single bony prominence), skin tears, and arterial/venous/diabetic ulcers. THIS PATIENT: the sacral wound is a STAGE 2 (partial-thickness, pink-red moist bed, no slough/fat), the heel is a DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (intact skin, non-blanchable deep maroon/purple), and the macerated sacral periwound skin is incontinence-associated dermatitis (a separate problem to treat, not staged as a pressure injury).
labs.r: Stage by deepest visible tissue: 1 = intact + NON-blanchable erythema; 2 = partial-thickness dermis (pink-red bed or serum blister, no slough/fat); 3 = full-thickness with fat (no bone/tendon/muscle); 4 = full-thickness with exposed bone/tendon/muscle; UNSTAGEABLE = base obscured by slough/eschar (don't debride STABLE dry heel eschar); DTPI = intact/non-intact skin, deep maroon/purple non-blanchable. NO back-staging (healing Stage 4 = 'healing Stage 4'). Distinguish from moisture-associated dermatitis. RN role: accurate staging + recognize DTPI/unstageable + heel-eschar exception.

labs.n: Braden risk assessment + the prevention bundle
labs.v: RISK ASSESSMENT with the BRADEN SCALE quantifies risk and DIRECTS prevention: six subscales — SENSORY PERCEPTION, MOISTURE, ACTIVITY, MOBILITY, NUTRITION, and FRICTION/SHEAR — where a LOWER total = HIGHER risk (≤18 at risk; ~15-18 mild, 13-14 moderate, 10-12 high, ≤9 very high). Assess on admission, with any condition change, and at regular intervals, and target interventions to the deficient subscales (this patient at 12 = HIGH risk, with deficits in mobility, activity, sensory perception, moisture, and nutrition). The comprehensive PREVENTION BUNDLE (pressure injuries are largely PREVENTABLE; hospital-acquired Stage 3/4 are serious reportable 'never' events): (1) SKIN ASSESSMENT head-to-toe on admission and at least daily and at each reposition, INCLUDING under/around medical devices; (2) REPOSITION at least EVERY 2 HOURS in bed and EVERY 1 HOUR in a chair (individualized turn schedule); (3) PRESSURE-REDISTRIBUTING SUPPORT SURFACES (specialty foam/alternating-pressure/low-air-loss mattress + chair cushion) — and NEVER use ring/'DONUT' cushions (they concentrate pressure and cause ischemia); (4) FLOAT THE HEELS off the bed (pillow lengthwise under the calves to suspend the heels — pressure-redistributing surfaces alone do not reliably offload heels); (5) keep the HEAD OF BED ≤30 degrees when feasible (and avoid prolonged elevation/slumped sitting) to reduce SHEAR; (6) MOISTURE management — prompt incontinence care, gentle pH-balanced cleansing, MOISTURE-BARRIER ointments/creams, and wicking/absorbent pads (treat incontinence-associated dermatitis); (7) minimize FRICTION/SHEAR — LIFT using draw/slide sheets and mechanical lifts rather than DRAGGING the patient, and use low-friction fabrics; (8) optimize NUTRITION + HYDRATION — adequate PROTEIN and calories, vitamins/minerals; involve a DIETITIAN (poor intake/low albumin impair skin integrity and healing); (9) early MOBILITY/activity with PT/OT as able; (10) do NOT MASSAGE reddened areas or bony prominences (it worsens underlying tissue damage); (11) pad/reposition/rotate MEDICAL DEVICES and inspect under them. THIS PATIENT received the full bundle: q2h repositioning (q1h in chair) on a turn schedule, a pressure-redistributing mattress/cushion (no donut), heels floated, HOB ≤30 degrees, lift-not-drag transfers, prompt incontinence care with a barrier cream, dietitian-led nutrition optimization, early mobility with PT/OT, and no massaging of bony prominences.
labs.r: BRADEN (6 subscales: sensory/moisture/activity/mobility/nutrition/friction-shear; LOWER = higher risk; ≤18 at risk) directs prevention. BUNDLE: daily + per-reposition skin checks (incl. under devices); reposition q2h bed / q1h chair; pressure-redistributing surfaces (NO donut cushions); FLOAT HEELS; HOB ≤30 (reduce shear); moisture management + barrier creams; LIFT don't drag; protein/calorie nutrition + dietitian; early mobility; DON'T massage bony prominences; protect under devices. RN role: Braden-targeted comprehensive prevention.

labs.v: WOUND CARE is tailored to the stage, wound bed, and exudate, and rests on two pillars — a MOIST WOUND ENVIRONMENT and PRESSURE OFFLOADING (a wound will NOT heal under continued pressure): (1) CLEANSE with normal saline or a noncytotoxic wound cleanser at each dressing change; do NOT routinely apply CYTOTOXIC agents (full-strength hydrogen peroxide, Dakin's solution, povidone-iodine) to healthy granulating tissue (they impair healing); (2) maintain a MOIST wound bed while keeping the PERIWOUND skin DRY/protected (skin barrier) — moist wound healing promotes granulation/epithelialization; (3) SELECT DRESSINGS by stage/exudate/depth: transparent FILM or HYDROCOLLOID for Stage 1-2 and shallow wounds; HYDROCOLLOID or FOAM for Stage 2-3; HYDROGEL to add moisture to DRY wounds; calcium ALGINATE or FOAM for HEAVY exudate; fillers/packing (e.g., alginate/gauze ribbon) for cavities, undermining, or tunneling (lightly fill dead space — do not pack tightly); (4) DEBRIDE nonviable tissue (slough/eschar) for Stage 3/4 and unstageable wounds — sharp (clinician), enzymatic, autolytic (occlusive dressings), or mechanical — to enable healing and accurate staging, with the KEY EXCEPTION that STABLE, dry, intact heel/ischemic-limb eschar is NOT debrided; (5) INFECTION — assess for local infection (increasing erythema/warmth/edema, purulence, malodor, increased pain, friable/discolored granulation, delayed healing) and systemic signs, and for OSTEOMYELITIS when bone is exposed/palpable; manage per orders (cultures, antibiotics, possible surgical consult); (6) manage EXUDATE (right dressing absorbency) and PAIN (pre-medicate for dressing changes; offloading reduces pain); (7) NUTRITION drives healing — ensure adequate PROTEIN, calories, fluids, and micronutrients (and treat the deficits identified by the dietitian); (8) involve a WOUND/OSTOMY/CONTINENCE (WOC) nurse and specialists (surgery/plastics, ID, nutrition) as needed; (9) DOCUMENT precisely at baseline and on regular reassessment: location, STAGE, dimensions (length x width x DEPTH in cm), undermining/tunneling (with clock positions), wound-bed tissue composition (% granulation/slough/eschar/epithelial), exudate amount/type/color, periwound skin, odor, and pain; MEASURE and PHOTOGRAPH per policy. CONTINUE all PREVENTION measures during treatment, and remember NOT to reverse-stage as the wound heals. THIS PATIENT: the sacral Stage 2 is cleansed with saline and dressed to maintain a moist bed (hydrocolloid/foam) with periwound protection and offloading; the heel DTPI is offloaded/floated, protected, and closely monitored (it may evolve); cytotoxic cleansers are avoided; nutrition is optimized; and the WOC nurse co-manages with thorough documentation.
labs.r: Wound care = MOIST wound bed + protect periwound + OFFLOAD pressure (won't heal under pressure). Cleanse with saline (avoid cytotoxic agents on healthy tissue); dressings by stage/exudate (film/hydrocolloid stage 1-2; foam/hydrocolloid 2-3; hydrogel for dry; alginate/foam for heavy exudate; fillers for cavities). DEBRIDE slough/eschar for stage 3/4/unstageable EXCEPT stable dry heel eschar. Assess infection/osteomyelitis; optimize NUTRITION (protein); WOC nurse; document size/depth/tissue/exudate + measure/photograph; no back-staging. RN role: stage-appropriate moist wound care + offloading + infection/nutrition + documentation.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice + accurate findings): the sacral wound is correctly identified as a STAGE 2 (partial-thickness; NOT a Stage 3 — no fat visible; NOT a moisture lesion — it is over the sacral bony prominence), the heel is correctly identified as a DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (intact skin with deep non-blanchable maroon/purple — NOT a simple Stage 1 erythema, and NOT yet unstageable), and the macerated perineal/sacral skin is recognized as INCONTINENCE-ASSOCIATED DERMATITIS (treated with moisture management/barrier — NOT staged as a pressure injury); the nurse did NOT MASSAGE the reddened/bony areas (which worsens damage); did NOT use a DONUT/ring cushion (causes ischemia); did NOT leave the HEELS resting on the bed (they are floated); did NOT apply cytotoxic cleansers (peroxide/Dakin's/povidone-iodine) to the healing Stage 2 bed; did NOT debride any STABLE dry heel eschar (none present, but the principle is held); and did NOT 'back-stage' the wounds as they heal. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: early, accurate skin assessment + STAGING + Braden risk identification; a comprehensive PREVENTION bundle (repositioning, pressure-redistributing surfaces, heel floating, HOB ≤30, moisture + friction/shear control); aggressive NUTRITION optimization (dietitian) + continence management; stage-appropriate moist wound care with OFFLOADING; WOC-nurse and interdisciplinary (PT/OT, dietitian) involvement; early MOBILITY; and thorough documentation enabling tracking. EXPECTED outcome (as here): with consistent prevention + treatment, the Stage 2 sacral injury heals (clean granulating, shrinking), the heel DTPI remains stable without progression, NO new injuries develop, and function/nutrition improve — whereas inconsistent offloading/repositioning, unmanaged moisture/malnutrition, and failure to float the heels would predictably allow the sacral wound to deepen to Stage 3/4 and the heel DTPI to evolve to a full-thickness/unstageable wound (largely PREVENTABLE harm). NURSING ROLE: assess skin + Braden risk, stage accurately (recognizing DTPI/unstageable, the stable-heel-eschar exception, and no back-staging), implement comprehensive prevention, deliver stage-appropriate moist wound care with offloading, prevent/detect infection, optimize nutrition + moisture + mobility, and coordinate/document care to prevent new injuries and promote healing.
labs.r: Negatives confirm correct practice: accurate staging (Stage 2 sacral; heel DTPI; perineal moisture lesion = IAD not a pressure injury), no massaging bony prominences, no donut cushion, heels floated, no cytotoxic cleansers, don't debride stable heel eschar, no back-staging. Protective: early staging + Braden + comprehensive prevention + nutrition + offloading + moist wound care + WOC/interdisciplinary + mobility + documentation → healing + no progression/new injuries. RN role: stage + prevent + treat + optimize + document.

nursesNote: Rehab/medical unit, ~2 weeks post ischemic stroke (left hemiparesis) — 78 y/o woman M. Sokolov with clustered pressure-injury RISK FACTORS: immobility (bed/chair-bound, needs assist to reposition), impaired sensation/awareness (affected side), urinary INCONTINENCE (moisture), POOR intake + weight loss (nutrition), friction/shear with transfers, advanced age. SKIN ASSESSMENT + STAGING: SACRAL = partial-thickness shallow open ulcer, pink-red MOIST bed, no slough/eschar, no visible fat → STAGE 2; right HEEL = intact skin, persistent NON-BLANCHABLE deep maroon/purple → DEEP TISSUE PRESSURE INJURY (DTPI); macerated sacral/perineal skin from incontinence = incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD — a separate moisture problem, NOT staged as a pressure injury). BRADEN = 12 (HIGH risk); low subscales for mobility/activity/sensory/moisture/nutrition + friction-shear → prevention targeted to each deficit. PREVENTION BUNDLE: reposition q2h in bed + q1h in chair (turn schedule); pressure-redistributing mattress + chair cushion (NO donut/ring — causes ischemia); HEELS FLOATED off the bed (pillow lengthwise under calves); HOB ≤30 degrees to reduce SHEAR; LIFT (draw sheet/mechanical lift) not DRAG; prompt incontinence care + pH-balanced cleanse + MOISTURE-BARRIER cream; dietitian for PROTEIN/calorie/fluid optimization; early mobility with PT/OT; did NOT massage reddened/bony areas; checked under/around medical devices; head-to-toe skin assessment daily + at each reposition. WOUND CARE (stage-appropriate, moist + offloaded): SACRAL Stage 2 — cleanse with normal saline (no cytotoxic agents on healthy tissue), maintain MOIST bed with hydrocolloid/foam dressing, protect periwound skin (barrier), OFFLOAD; HEEL DTPI — OFFLOAD/float + protect + monitor closely (may evolve rapidly); no debridement indicated (no stable eschar; and stable dry heel eschar would NOT be debrided); WOC nurse co-managing; DOCUMENTED location/stage/size (L x W x DEPTH)/undermining/tissue %/exudate/periwound/odor/pain + measured + photographed per policy; no back-staging as wounds heal. DAY 7: sacral Stage 2 improving (clean granulating, shrinking) with offloading + moist wound care + improved nutrition + continence management; heel DTPI stable/intact, no progression with consistent heel offloading; NO new injuries; Braden interventions sustained; weight/intake improving. CONTRAST (teaching): without consistent repositioning/offloading + moisture/nutrition management + heel floating, the sacral Stage 2 could deepen to Stage 3/4 and the heel DTPI rapidly evolve to unstageable/full-thickness — largely PREVENTABLE, serious reportable hospital-acquired harm. Pressure-injury care = core Basic Care & Comfort (skin integrity, hygiene, nutrition, mobility, comfort).
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: rehab/medical unit; post-ischemic-stroke (left hemiparesis) with high pressure-injury risk; sacral Stage 2 pressure injury + right-heel deep tissue pressure injury
DIAGNOSIS: Stage 2 sacral pressure injury + right-heel deep tissue pressure injury; incontinence-associated dermatitis; high Braden risk (12)
CODE STATUS: full code; stroke recovery
ASSESSMENT + STAGING:
Head-to-toe skin assessment on admission, at least daily, and at each reposition (include under/around medical devices)

Stage injuries by deepest visible tissue (1 = non-blanchable intact; 2 = partial-thickness dermis; 3 = fat visible; 4 = bone/tendon/muscle; unstageable = obscured by slough/eschar; DTPI = deep non-blanchable maroon/purple); do NOT reverse/back-stage healing wounds
Braden Scale on admission + regularly; target interventions to deficient subscales
Distinguish pressure injury from incontinence-associated dermatitis/moisture lesions, skin tears, and other ulcers
PREVENTION BUNDLE:
Reposition at least q2h in bed and q1h in chair (individualized turn schedule)
Pressure-redistributing support surfaces (mattress + chair cushion); do NOT use ring/donut cushions
Float heels off the bed (pillow under calves); keep head of bed at or below 30 degrees when feasible (reduce shear)
Lift (draw/slide sheet, mechanical lift) rather than drag; use low-friction textiles
Moisture management: prompt incontinence care, gentle cleansing, moisture-barrier products, wicking pads; treat incontinence-associated dermatitis
Do NOT massage reddened areas/bony prominences; pad and reposition medical devices
Early mobility/activity with PT/OT as able
NUTRITION:
Dietitian consult; optimize protein, calories, fluids, and micronutrients; monitor intake/weight/labs (e.g., prealbumin per protocol) — malnutrition impairs healing
WOUND CARE (stage-appropriate; maintain moist bed + offload pressure):
Cleanse with normal saline/noncytotoxic cleanser; avoid cytotoxic agents (full-strength peroxide/Dakin's/povidone-iodine) on healthy granulating tissue
Sacral Stage 2: maintain moist wound bed with appropriate dressing (hydrocolloid/foam) + protect periwound skin + offload
Heel deep tissue pressure injury: offload/float + protect + monitor closely (may evolve)
Select dressings by stage/exudate/depth (film/hydrocolloid for shallow; foam/hydrocolloid for moderate; hydrogel for dry; alginate/foam for heavy exudate; fillers for cavities/undermining)
Debride nonviable tissue for Stage 3/4/unstageable wounds EXCEPT stable dry intact heel/ischemic eschar (do not debride)
Assess for local infection/osteomyelitis and exudate; cultures/antibiotics/surgical consult per orders; manage wound pain (premedicate for dressing changes)

WOC (wound/ostomy/continence) nurse consult; document location/stage/dimensions (L x W x depth)/undermining/tunneling/tissue %/exudate/periwound/odor/pain; measure + photograph per policy; reassess regularly
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + MONITORING:
WOC nurse + dietitian + PT/OT + provider; continence management; monitor for new injuries + healing trajectory
Continue prevention measures throughout treatment; educate patient/family on repositioning, skin checks, nutrition, and offloading for discharge

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this immobile, high-risk patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Clustered risk factors after stroke — immobility, impaired sensation, incontinence (moisture), poor nutrition/weight loss, friction/shear, and advanced age — markedly increase pressure-injury risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the risk-factor cluster drives prevention. RN role: identify risk + target prevention.

opts.text: The sacral wound (partial-thickness, pink-red moist bed, no slough/fat) is a Stage 2 pressure injury; correct staging guides care
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — accurate staging (Stage 2) directs wound care. RN role: stage correctly.

opts.text: The heel area of intact skin with persistent non-blanchable deep maroon/purple discoloration is a deep tissue pressure injury (DTPI) that may evolve rapidly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing DTPI (not simple erythema) prompts aggressive offloading + monitoring. RN role: identify DTPI + offload/monitor.

opts.text: Perform a Braden risk assessment and target interventions to the deficient subscales (mobility, activity, sensory, moisture, nutrition, friction/shear)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Braden quantifies + directs prevention. RN role: Braden assessment + targeted care.

opts.text: Implement a prevention bundle — reposition q2h (q1h in chair), pressure-redistributing surfaces, float heels, HOB ≤30 degrees, moisture management, lift-not-drag, nutrition optimization
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bundle prevents progression + new injuries. RN role: comprehensive prevention.

opts.text: Provide stage-appropriate moist wound care with pressure OFFLOADING and protect the periwound skin; the wound won't heal under continued pressure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — moist wound healing + offloading are the pillars of healing. RN role: stage-appropriate wound care + offload.

opts.text: Optimize nutrition/hydration (protein, calories; dietitian) and manage incontinence/moisture; involve a WOC nurse and document/measure precisely
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — nutrition + moisture + WOC + documentation support healing/tracking. RN role: nutrition/moisture + WOC + documentation.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant for dressings/topicals. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Vigorously massage the reddened sacral area and bony prominences each shift to improve circulation and prevent breakdown
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Massaging reddened areas/bony prominences over compromised tissue WORSENS damage and is contraindicated. Relieve pressure (reposition/offload), don't massage.

opts.text: Place a ring/donut cushion under the sacrum and leave the heels resting on the mattress to keep her comfortable
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Donut/ring cushions concentrate pressure and cause ischemia (contraindicated), and heels must be FLOATED off the surface — leaving them on the mattress promotes heel injury.

opts.text: Stage 1 = intact skin with non-blanchable erythema; Stage 2 = partial-thickness dermis (pink-red bed or serum-filled blister, no slough/fat); Stage 3 = fat visible; Stage 4 = exposed bone/tendon/muscle
opts.rat: CORRECT — the staging framework. Foundation for accurate staging.

opts.text: Unstageable = full-thickness loss with the base obscured by slough/eschar; a deep tissue pressure injury = intact/non-intact skin with deep non-blanchable maroon/purple discoloration
opts.rat: CORRECT — unstageable + DTPI definitions. Foundation for recognizing these special categories.

opts.text: Stable, dry, intact eschar on the heel (or an ischemic limb) should NOT be debrided — it serves as the body's natural biological cover
opts.rat: CORRECT — the stable-heel-eschar exception. Foundation for safe debridement decisions.

opts.text: Pressure injuries are not reverse/back-staged as they heal (a healing Stage 4 is documented as a 'healing Stage 4'), because granulation/scar replaces the lost tissue
opts.rat: CORRECT — the no-back-staging principle. Foundation for accurate documentation.

opts.text: The Braden Scale (6 subscales) quantifies risk with lower scores indicating higher risk, and prevention is directed to the deficient subscales
opts.rat: CORRECT — Braden risk principle. Foundation for targeted prevention.

opts.text: Wound healing requires a moist wound bed AND offloading of pressure; cytotoxic cleansers should not be routinely used on healthy granulating tissue
opts.rat: CORRECT — moist healing + offloading + avoid-cytotoxic principle. Foundation for wound care.

opts.text: Massaging reddened bony prominences and using donut/ring cushions are recommended pressure-injury prevention measures
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Both are contraindicated — massaging worsens tissue damage, and donut cushions cause ischemia. Reposition/offload with pressure-redistributing surfaces instead.

opts.text: Incontinence-associated dermatitis over the perineum/skin folds should be staged using the pressure-injury staging system
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Moisture-associated/incontinence dermatitis is NOT a pressure injury and is not staged with the pressure-injury system; it is managed with moisture control and barriers (though it does increase pressure-injury risk).

opts.text: Relieve pressure — reposition on schedule, offload the sacral wound and float the heels, and use pressure-redistributing surfaces (the wound won't heal, and new injuries will form, under continued pressure)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Pressure relief/offloading is the foundation of both prevention and healing. RN role: reposition + offload.

opts.text: Assess + stage accurately and perform a Braden risk assessment to guide individualized care
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Accurate staging + risk assessment direct the whole plan. RN role: stage + Braden.

opts.text: Provide stage-appropriate moist wound care with periwound protection and infection surveillance
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Correct wound care promotes healing + detects infection. RN role: moist wound care + monitor.

opts.text: Optimize nutrition/hydration (dietitian) and manage moisture/incontinence
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Nutrition + moisture control are essential for healing/prevention. RN role: nutrition + moisture management.

opts.text: Coordinate WOC/interdisciplinary care, document/measure precisely, and educate the patient/family for discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Important + ongoing. Coordination, documentation, and teaching sustain outcomes. RN role: WOC/coordination + documentation + education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: Reposition at least every 2 hours in bed (every 1 hour in the chair) on a turn schedule, using pressure-redistributing surfaces and lift-not-drag transfers
opts.rat: Core pressure-relief/prevention. RN role: scheduled repositioning + redistribution + safe transfers.

opts.text: Float the heels off the bed with a pillow under the calves and keep the head of bed at or below 30 degrees to reduce shear
opts.rat: Offloads heels + reduces shear. RN role: heel floating + HOB control.

opts.text: Provide stage-appropriate moist wound care (saline cleanse, hydrocolloid/foam for the Stage 2 bed), protect periwound skin, and offload the wound
opts.rat: Moist wound healing + offloading + periwound protection. RN role: stage-appropriate wound care.

opts.text: Manage incontinence/moisture (prompt care, gentle cleansing, moisture-barrier cream) and optimize nutrition/hydration with a dietitian
opts.rat: Moisture + nutrition are key to prevention/healing. RN role: moisture management + nutrition.

opts.text: Offload and closely monitor the heel deep tissue pressure injury, recognizing it may evolve, and involve a WOC nurse with precise documentation
opts.rat: Appropriate DTPI management + WOC + documentation. RN role: offload/monitor DTPI + coordinate/document.

opts.text: Perform a Braden risk assessment and head-to-toe skin checks (including under devices), targeting prevention to deficient subscales
opts.rat: Risk assessment + skin surveillance direct care. RN role: Braden + skin assessment.

opts.text: Vigorously massage the reddened sacral area and bony prominences to stimulate circulation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — massaging compromised tissue over bony prominences worsens damage and is contraindicated. Relieve pressure instead.

opts.text: Use a ring/donut cushion under the sacrum to relieve pressure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — donut/ring cushions concentrate pressure and cause ischemia; use pressure-redistributing surfaces instead.

opts.text: Apply full-strength hydrogen peroxide or povidone-iodine to the clean granulating Stage 2 wound bed at each change
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — cytotoxic agents damage healthy granulating tissue and impair healing; cleanse with normal saline/noncytotoxic cleanser.

opts.text: Sharply debride the patient's stable, dry, intact heel eschar should one be present, to 'speed healing'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stable, dry, intact heel/ischemic eschar is the body's protective cover and should NOT be debrided (debriding it risks deeper injury/infection).

opts.text: As the wound heals, reverse-stage it (e.g., relabel a healing deeper wound as a Stage 1/2) in the documentation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — pressure injuries are not reverse/back-staged; a healing deeper injury is documented as 'healing Stage X' because granulation/scar replaces the lost tissue.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Relieve/offload pressure now — reposition, offload the sacrum, float the heels, and ensure pressure-redistributing surfaces are in place
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate pressure relief stops ongoing tissue injury and is foundational. RN role: offload/reposition.

opts.text: Complete a head-to-toe skin assessment + accurate staging + Braden risk assessment
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Assessment/staging/risk direct the individualized plan. RN role: assess + stage + Braden.

opts.text: Provide stage-appropriate moist wound care with periwound protection and infection surveillance
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver the wound treatment once offloading + assessment are set. RN role: wound care.

opts.text: Optimize nutrition/hydration (dietitian) and manage incontinence/moisture
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Nutrition + moisture control support healing/prevention. RN role: nutrition + moisture.

opts.text: Coordinate WOC/interdisciplinary care, document/measure precisely, and educate the patient/family
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing. Coordination + documentation + teaching sustain the plan. RN role: WOC/coordination + documentation + education.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The nurse staged the sacral wound as Stage 2 and the heel as a DTPI, performed a Braden assessment (12), and initiated the full prevention bundle with stage-appropriate wound care
opts.rat: Accurate staging + risk assessment + comprehensive prevention/treatment. Model handling that sets up healing.

opts.text: With q2h repositioning, heel floating, pressure-redistributing surfaces, moisture management, nutrition optimization, and moist wound care, the Stage 2 sacral wound is granulating and shrinking
opts.rat: Consistent prevention + treatment producing healing. Expected favorable trajectory.

opts.text: The heel deep tissue pressure injury remains stable with intact skin and no progression under consistent offloading/floating
opts.rat: Effective offloading kept the DTPI from evolving — a good outcome for a lesion that often deteriorates. Improving/stable trajectory.

opts.text: By day 7 there are no new pressure injuries, weight/intake are improving with the dietitian's plan, and the patient is more mobile with PT/OT
opts.rat: Prevention working (no new injuries) + improving nutrition/mobility. Strong outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the patient was left in the same position for long periods, heels rested on the mattress, incontinence and poor intake went unaddressed; the sacral wound deepened to expose subcutaneous fat (Stage 3) and the heel evolved to an unstageable wound
opts.rat: ⚠️ Failed offloading/repositioning + unmanaged moisture/nutrition allowed progression (Stage 2 → 3; DTPI → unstageable). Demonstrates the harm of inconsistent prevention.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff massaged the reddened bony prominences and placed a donut cushion under the sacrum; the tissue damage worsened and a new ischemic injury formed
opts.rat: ⚠️ Massaging bony prominences + donut cushions worsen/cause ischemic injury. Demonstrates these contraindicated practices.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — full-strength povidone-iodine and peroxide were applied to the clean Stage 2 bed at every change; granulation tissue was damaged and healing stalled
opts.rat: ⚠️ Cytotoxic agents on healthy granulating tissue impair healing. Demonstrates why noncytotoxic cleansing (saline) is used.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a stable, dry, intact heel eschar (in a different patient) was sharply debrided, exposing deeper tissue that became infected
opts.rat: ⚠️ Debriding stable dry heel eschar removes the protective biological cover and risks deeper injury/infection. Demonstrates the stable-heel-eschar exception.

opts.text: Shortly after the prevention bundle is started, the Stage 2 sacral wound looks essentially the same on the next assessment, with no new injuries and the wound bed clean
opts.rat: Wound healing takes time; an unchanged, clean, non-progressing wound shortly after starting care is acceptable/expected — continue the plan and reassess for healing.

opts.text: Weeks later, with sustained prevention + nutrition, the sacral injury has healed and the patient is repositioning herself with improving mobility, with no recurrence
opts.rat: Sustained healing + restored mobility + no recurrence. Standard ideal long-term outcome.
